aids.title: Reverse: S-R-M-M
aids.content:
**S**ave next, **R**everse link, **M**ove prev, **M**ove current.

next = curr->next; curr->next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next;
aids.forRemembering: The four steps to reverse a singly linked list iteratively

aids.title: Slow-Fast = Tortoise-Hare
aids.content: Like the fable of the tortoise and hare! The slow tortoise moves 1 step while the fast hare moves 2 steps. When the hare finishes the race (reaches end of list), the tortoise is at the middle.
aids.forRemembering: Two-pointer technique for finding the middle element

aids.title: Cycle Detection Logic
aids.content: If there is a cycle, the fast pointer will 'lap' the slow pointer eventually. Like runners on a circular track -- the faster one always catches up if the track is circular.
aids.forRemembering: Why Floyd's cycle detection algorithm works

aids.title: Head = O(1), Everywhere Else = O(n)
aids.content: Operations at the HEAD are O(1) because we have direct access. Everything else (insert at end, delete at end, search) requires traversal and is O(n). This is the key trade-off of singly linked lists.
aids.forRemembering: Time complexity pattern for singly linked list operations
